region_of_origin: Southwest Asia / Caucasus (widely cultivated in Mediterranean, Middle East, and temperate regions)

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium raw quince (approximately 150g) is a typical single serving, eaten raw or used in cooking and preserves.

nutrient_source.title: Quince, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 09296)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values from HPB Singapore Foodo for quince (raw). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09296 (Quince, raw) for saturated fat, sugar, and micronutrient detail. Values are consistent between both sources.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Fresh Fruits — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for low-GI fresh fruits (pear GI 38, apple GI 36); quince estimated at GI 35 given similar carbohydrate composition, high tannin content, and firm texture reducing starch digestibility rate.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for raw quince found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on closest analogous fruits: pear (GI 38, Sydney GI DB) and apple (GI 36, Sydney GI DB). Quince shares similar carbohydrate density (15.3g/100g), high polyphenol and tannin content, and firm dense texture that slows gastric emptying and glucose absorption. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 22.95) / 100 ≈ 8.0 for a 150g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (4mg/100g) with meaningful potassium (197mg/100g) supporting favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Raw quince is suitable for hypertension management; its negligible sodium content and moderate potassium level support blood pressure regulation, and the whole-fruit sugar (6.3g/100g) with fibre (1.9g/100g) does not trigger the solid-food sugar concern threshold — avoid quince jam or jelly which contains very high added sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (15.3g/100g) with low estimated GI (~35) and polyphenol content modulating post-meal glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw quince has an estimated low GI of approximately 35 and a glycaemic load of ~8 per 150g serving, making it appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions (one medium fruit); avoid quince jam, jelly, or preparations with added sugar which substantially raise glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.006g/100g), zero cholesterol, and polyphenol antioxidants (quercetin, chlorogenic acid) with dietary fibre supporting lipid management
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw quince is cardioprotective in profile — its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, moderate dietary fibre (1.9g/100g), and rich polyphenol content (quercetin, chlorogenic acid, tannins) support cardiovascular health; consume as whole fresh fruit rather than processed quince products with added sugar or syrup.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Polyphenol content (tannins, chlorogenic acid, quercetin) and vitamin C (15mg/100g) provide antioxidant support, but fibre at 1.9g/100g is below the 3g whole-food fibre threshold for positive RA classification
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw quince may offer modest anti-inflammatory benefit via its polyphenol and vitamin C content, but fibre is insufficient to meet the positive RA anti-inflammatory threshold; avoid quince jam or jelly as high added sugar is pro-inflammatory, and prefer poaching with no added sugar if cooking.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Quince (Cydonia oblonga) is a golden-yellow, pear-shaped fruit with a firm, dense, and astringent flesh that is rarely eaten raw due to its intensely tart and tannic taste. It is one of the oldest cultivated fruits, originating in the Caucasus and Southwest Asia, and is widely used in Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, and European cuisines. Raw quince provides 57 kcal per 100g, with 15.3g carbohydrate, 1.9g dietary fibre, 0.4g protein, and only 0.1g fat. Its sodium content is negligible at 4mg/100g, while potassium is 197mg/100g, making it favourable for blood pressure management. Quince is rich in polyphenols including tannins, chlorogenic acid, and quercetin, which contribute to its antioxidant and potential anti-inflammatory properties. Vitamin C content is approximately 15mg/100g. The estimated glycaemic index is low (~35), comparable to pear and apple, making it suitable for blood glucose management when consumed as whole fruit. Quince is most commonly cooked — poached, baked, or made into jam, jelly, paste (membrillo), or marmalade — as heat transforms its flesh from white and hard to a soft, rose-pink colour with a floral, honey-like aroma. For clinical use, raw or minimally sweetened cooked quince is appropriate for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease management; processed quince products with high added sugar should be avoided in all three conditions.
common_user_queries: Can I eat quince if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycaemic index of quince?
Is quince suitable for a low-calorie diet?

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary approved source) with cross-validation against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09296 (Quince, raw). Both sources are consistent. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for structurally similar low-GI fruits (pear, apple) and is clearly documented as an estimate.
notes: Saturated fat (0.006g/100g), monounsaturated fat (0.037g/100g), and polyunsaturated fat (0.051g/100g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 09296 (Quince, raw) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown. Sugar value of 6.3g/100g sourced from USDA SR Legacy cross-reference. Vitamin C (15mg/100g), vitamin A (2mcg RAE), B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water content sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 09296. Cholesterol is confirmed zero (naturally zero — plant food contains no cholesterol). Vitamin B12 and vitamin D are naturally zero in plant-based whole fruit. Trans fat is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate — no direct GI measurement for raw quince exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026; estimate based on pear (GI 38) and apple (GI 36) from the same database. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, B-vitamin values, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are populated from USDA SR Legacy as supplementary detail not available in HPB source.
